title: Indexes
content:
### SQL Indexes

An index is a database structure that improves the speed of data retrieval operations on a table at the cost of additional storage and slower writes.

---

### Creating an Index

```sql
-- Simple index on one column
CREATE INDEX idx_employee_name ON employees(name);

-- Unique index (prevents duplicate values)
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_unique_email ON employees(email);

-- Composite index on multiple columns
CREATE INDEX idx_dept_salary ON employees(department, salary);
```

### How Indexes Work

Similar to a book index, a database index maintains a sorted list of values with pointers to the actual rows. This allows the database to find rows without scanning the entire table.

### When to Use Indexes

| Good for Indexes | Bad for Indexes |
|-----------------|----------------|
| Columns used in WHERE | Columns rarely queried |
| JOIN columns (foreign keys) | Small tables (less than 1000 rows) |
| Columns with high cardinality | Columns with few distinct values (e.g., gender) |
| ORDER BY and GROUP BY columns | Columns frequently updated |

### Index Types

| Type | Description |
|------|-------------|
| B-Tree | Default, balanced tree for general use |
| Hash | Fast equality lookups, no range queries |
| Bitmap | Good for low-cardinality columns |
| Full-Text | Text search optimization |
| Spatial | Geographic data optimization |

### Dropping an Index

```sql
DROP INDEX IF EXISTS idx_employee_name;

-- MySQL
DROP INDEX idx_employee_name ON employees;
```

> **Key Point:** Indexes speed up SELECT but slow down INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE. Use them judiciously on frequently queried columns.
codeExample:
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_customer_date
ON orders(customer_id, order_date);
